input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 1: Density as a function of aluminum content for binary Fe-Al and quaternary 0.2C-8.5Mn alloys. Density decreases linearly with Al content; experimental data for quaternary alloys slightly lower than binary. Thermo-Calc calculation matched well at low Al, deviation at high Al possibly due to kappa-phase not considered.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Density reduction of 8–10% or higher is achieved versus low-carbon steels by aluminum additions; binary Fe-Al density decreases from ~7.25 g/cm³ at 6% Al to ~6.55 g/cm³ at 12.5% Al.

input_candidates.truth_value: Hall-Petch relation for 28.8Mn-9.4Al-0.92C alloy: yield strength coefficient 461 MPa·μm^{1/2}, tensile strength coefficient 351 MPa·μm^{1/2}.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Quaternary 0.2C-8.5Mn (wt.%) alloys studied in this work show slightly lower density than binary Fe-Al by picnometry; continuous line in Fig. 1 corresponds to Thermo-Calc density calculation.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 10: Influence of annealing temperature and time on tensile strength of 8.5Mn-0.2C based alloys; strength decreases with increasing temperature/time, with a relatively small change in MPa/°C.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Dynamic recrystallization map for Fe-8% Al ferritic steel (Fig. 6): DDRX is activated at strain rates ≥0.5 s⁻¹ for 900°C, ≥0.5 s⁻¹ for 1000°C, and ≥0.1 s⁻¹ for 1100°C; below these thresholds DDRX is not activated.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 12: Engineering stress-strain curves for fully austenitic 0.92C-28.8Mn-9.4Al alloy with grain sizes from 1.65 to 40 μm. Table data extracted from figure.

input_candidates.truth_value: Dynamic recrystallization map (Fig. 6) for Fe-8Al ferritic steel: DDRX activated at high temperature and high strain rate, CDRX at lower temperature and lower strain rate.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Carbon content effect on yield strength in austenitic grades: decreasing C leads to a significant strength decrease, quantified as lower than 300 MPa per wt.% C but certainly higher than 187 MPa per wt.% C.

input_candidates.truth_value: Roping defects in ferritic Fe-Al alloys attributed to inhomogeneous grain orientations; DDRX preferred to limit roping.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Ferrite fraction effect on mechanical properties in austenitic grades: 2–5% ferrite increases yield strength by 30–50 MPa; above 30% ferrite yield strength decreases; ultimate tensile strength decreases by ~80 MPa when adding up to 39% ferrite; total elongation decreases 6% with only 3% ferrite and up to 16% with 39% ferrite.

candidates.truth_value: Roping defects in ferritic Fe-Al alloys attributed to inhomogeneous grain orientations; DDRX preferred to limit roping.
candidates.prediction_value: Ferrite fraction effect on mechanical properties in austenitic grades: 2–5% ferrite increases yield strength by 30–50 MPa; above 30% ferrite yield strength decreases; ultimate tensile strength decreases by ~80 MPa when adding up to 39% ferrite; total elongation decreases 6% with only 3% ferrite and up to 16% with 39% ferrite.
